title: Labor in the Gilded Age
description: Practice your skills in this exercise on labor in the Gilded Age.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below.

>“As America steadily became more urban and as real incomes rose, the share of food and clothing produced at home declined sharply, freeing some of the time previously engaged in household production. New types of processed food were invented, and many of today’s name brands became commonplace in the last three decades of the nineteenth century.”

>-Source: Robert J. Gordon, historian, *The Rise and Fall of American Growth: The U.S. Standard of Living Since the Civil War,* 2016

**The excerpt best illustrates which of the following developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the effects of industrialization
the expansion into foreign markets
the growth of labor unions
the mass migration to rural areas

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below.

>“As America steadily became more urban and as real incomes rose, the share of food and clothing produced at home declined sharply, freeing some of the time previously engaged in household production. New types of processed food were invented, and many of today’s name brands became commonplace in the last three decades of the nineteenth century.”

>-Source: Robert J. Gordon, historian, *The Rise and Fall of American Growth: The U.S. Standard of Living Since the Civil War,* 2016

**According to the passage, which of the following best explains the most important effect that the rise in incomes and relocation to cities had on the economy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It led to the transition toward purchasing mass-produced household goods.
It caused a financial panic as business owners could not support their workers.
It generated a gap between the wealthy employers and the poorer employees.
It prompted backlash from farmers in rural areas who struggled to sell goods.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below.

>“As America steadily became more urban and as real incomes rose, the share of food and clothing produced at home declined sharply, freeing some of the time previously engaged in household production. New types of processed food were invented, and many of today’s name brands became commonplace in the last three decades of the nineteenth century.”

>-Source: Robert J. Gordon, historian, *The Rise and Fall of American Growth: The U.S. Standard of Living Since the Civil War,* 2016

**Which of the following is one important continuity in American life in the United States throughout the Gilded Age?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the standard of living improved
the majority of Americans lived in cities
the middle class disappeared
the real wages of workers decreased

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below.

>“The inequality between the millionaire capitalist and his [employee] on the verge of pauperism, on the verge of starvation is greater than the difference between the millionaire and his wife, and the [employee] and his wife. The mere demand for equality of the sexes is fine as far as it goes, but to make the working woman the mere equal of the working man in these days of wage reductions, lock-outs, short-time. . . and the rest of the workingman’s hardships, is a task which will not arouse much enthusiasm among thinking people. . . . In the absence of a program, the first principle of suffragists that occurs to one’s mind is always equality of the sexes. But, at present, the inequality of men among themselves is so frightful that to make each woman (rich or poor) merely equal to the men of her family, or her class, is to leave the sum total of inequality now cursing society almost unchanged.”

>-Source: Florence Kelley, in a letter to Clara Berwick Colby, 1885

**Which of the following issues of the period was the author most likely concerned with in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the growing wage gap between the rich and the poor
the formation of labor unions to protest unfair wages
the efforts to pass an amendment guaranteeing equal wages
the rise of a women’s rights movement to fight inequality

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below.

>“The inequality between the millionaire capitalist and his [employee] on the verge of pauperism, on the verge of starvation is greater than the difference between the millionaire and his wife, and the [employee] and his wife. The mere demand for equality of the sexes is fine as far as it goes, but to make the working woman the mere equal of the working man in these days of wage reductions, lock-outs, short-time. . . and the rest of the workingman’s hardships, is a task which will not arouse much enthusiasm among thinking people. . . . In the absence of a program, the first principle of suffragists that occurs to one’s mind is always equality of the sexes. But, at present, the inequality of men among themselves is so frightful that to make each woman (rich or poor) merely equal to the men of her family, or her class, is to leave the sum total of inequality now cursing society almost unchanged.”

>-Source: Florence Kelley, in a letter to Clara Berwick Colby, 1885

**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how people responded to inequalities in the Gilded Age
how women organized for the right to vote in all elections
how women struggled to find representation in labor unions
how women’s roles changed in response to income inequality

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below.

>“‘What can labor do for itself?’ The answer is not difficult. Labor can organize, it can unify, it can consolidate its forces. This done, it can demand and command. Such are the possible and the practical things labor can do, is doing, and will continue to do until constitutions and courts and laws based upon principles of eternal justice, make no distinction in dealing with the people.”

>-Eugene V. Debs, *Brotherhood of Locomotive Firemen and Enginemen's Magazine,* 1893

**The excerpt provided is best understood in the context of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the growing tension between employees and employers over wages and working conditions
the lack of public support for labor unions as the opposition portrayed them as anti-capitalist
the creation of new government regulations to limit the creation of trusts
the emergence of cooperative farming organizations calling for government regulation

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below.

>“‘What can labor do for itself?’ The answer is not difficult. Labor can organize, it can unify, it can consolidate its forces. This done, it can demand and command. Such are the possible and the practical things labor can do, is doing, and will continue to do until constitutions and courts and laws based upon principles of eternal justice, make no distinction in dealing with the people.”

>-Eugene V. Debs, *Brotherhood of Locomotive Firemen and Enginemen's Magazine,* 1893

**People who shared the author’s ideas at the time most typically sought to achieve their goals by doing which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: organizing strikes and bargaining through representatives
forming the People’s Party to influence policy decisions
marching on the White House to advocate for new policies
breaking machines at major factories to impede production

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below.

>“We want eight hours and nothing less. We have been accused of being selfish, and it has been said that we will want more; that last year we got an advance of ten cents and now we want more. We do want more. . . . 

>“In the age of electricity and steam that has produced wealth a hundred fold, we insist that it has been brought about by the intelligence and energy of the workingmen, and while we find that is now easier to produce it is harder to live. We do want more, and when it becomes more, we shall still want more. And we shall never cease to demand more until we have received the results of our labor.”

>Samuel Gompers, “Eights Hours and Nothing Less,” 1890

**The remarks in the excerpt were most likely given in response to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise of local and national labor organizations
the expansion of the labor force to include children
the beginning of a national financial panic
the replacement of workers with labor-saving machines

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below.

>“We want eight hours and nothing less. We have been accused of being selfish, and it has been said that we will want more; that last year we got an advance of ten cents and now we want more. We do want more. . . . 

>“In the age of electricity and steam that has produced wealth a hundred fold, we insist that it has been brought about by the intelligence and energy of the workingmen, and while we find that is now easier to produce it is harder to live. We do want more, and when it becomes more, we shall still want more. And we shall never cease to demand more until we have received the results of our labor.”

>Samuel Gompers, “Eights Hours and Nothing Less,” 1890

**Arguments similar to those expressed in the excerpt were later employed to justify which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: direct confrontations with their employers
realignment of political parties to address complaints
exclusion of children from the labor force
establishment of business regulations

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the cartoon and answer the question below.

![Political cartoon showing, on one side, an overseer whipping an enslaved man, and on the other side, a factory owner with a stopwatch glaring at a young woman as she enters the factory. The caption reads: "Uncle Sam stopped this horror more than forty years ago. Now he is grappling with this shocking evil of child mines, factories and sweat shops."](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3471fd31225955e488c3cf10b51f97fd.png)

*Political cartoon showing an overseer whipping an enslaved man in one panel and a factory owner with a stopwatch glaring at a young woman as she enters a factory in the other panel. The caption reads: "Uncle Sam stopped this horror more than forty years ago. Now he is grappling with this shocking evil of child mines, factories and sweat shops."*

**The image most closely reflects which of the following developments in the political climate in the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the expansion of the industrial workforce to include children
the rising wage inequalities between business owners and employees
the changing management structure to improve the organization of factories
the mistreatment of women in major industries and their lack of representation

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the cartoon and answer the question below.

![Political cartoon showing, on one side, an overseer whipping an enslaved man, and on the other side, a factory owner with a stopwatch glaring at a young woman as she enters the factory. The caption reads: "Uncle Sam stopped this horror more than forty years ago. Now he is grappling with this shocking evil of child mines, factories and sweat shops."](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3471fd31225955e488c3cf10b51f97fd.png)

*Political cartoon showing an overseer whipping an enslaved man in one panel and a factory owner with a stopwatch glaring at a young woman as she enters a factory in the other panel. The caption reads: "Uncle Sam stopped this horror more than forty years ago. Now he is grappling with this shocking evil of child mines, factories and sweat shops."*

**The image was created most directly in response to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the practice of using children as factory workers
the proposal of an amendment for women’s suffrage
the creation of settlement houses to support factory workers
the change in gender roles following industrialization

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the cartoon and answer the question below.

![Political cartoon showing, on one side, an overseer whipping an enslaved man, and on the other side, a factory owner with a stopwatch glaring at a young woman as she enters the factory. The caption reads: "Uncle Sam stopped this horror more than forty years ago. Now he is grappling with this shocking evil of child mines, factories and sweat shops."](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3471fd31225955e488c3cf10b51f97fd.png)

*Political cartoon showing an overseer whipping an enslaved man in one panel and a factory owner with a stopwatch glaring at a young woman as she enters a factory in the other panel. The caption reads: "Uncle Sam stopped this horror more than forty years ago. Now he is grappling with this shocking evil of child mines, factories and sweat shops."*

**The political cartoon was intended to:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: persuade the American public to regulate labor practices.
advocate for the re-enslavement of African Americans.
convince people to support women’s suffrage.
promote traditional gender roles for women.
